blurb:
A Good Samaritan spotted Caspian and made sure he got somewhere safe. He landed at Mostly Mutts, found a great foster home, and has been making himself very comfortable ever since. He's 8 months old, about 9.5 pounds, and still growing into himself.

His foster describes him as sweet and affectionate, and the specifics back that up. He wants to be near you. Lap sitting is his preferred activity, but he's equally happy playing when you're in the mood for it. He gets excited about treats. He keeps his litter box clean without being asked. He checks a lot of boxes without making a big deal about it.

He gets along well with the other cats in his foster home, so a multi-cat household could work well for him. How he does with dogs is unknown since he hasn't had the chance to meet one yet. He's been good with kids and does best when they're gentle and know how to handle a cat. Bring your crew in for a meet and greet before committing.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue based in Kennesaw. Visit their website to submit an inquiry about Caspian and schedule a visit.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
â¢ Lap-sitting, treat-loving, litter-box-reliable young cat with almost no adoption friction â broadest audience appeal
â¢ Good Samaritan rescue origin adds a feel-good hook without requiring a heavy backstory
â¢ Appears recently placed in foster with no urgency signals â lower priority for limited newsletter space

blurb:
Judy is a one-year-old tuxedo cat who's been living her best life in foster care, charming everyone she meets, and waiting patiently while her siblings all got adopted before her. She's got a sleek coat, a big purr, and a heart that literally has a tiny hole in it. That last part sounds more dramatic than it is. The condition is called a ventricular septal defect, and she's had two echocardiograms that show she's doing well. You'd never know anything was different about her watching her tear around with a feather wand.

She's playful and curious but not chaotic. She likes a lively home, just not a circus. When she's done running the show, she'll find a soft blanket and curl up. She also plays independently, so you don't have to entertain her every second.

Judy has met dogs, cats, and kids and handled all of it with confidence, though she does best when introductions are slow and she gets to come around on her own terms. She gave a dog the nose-to-nose sniff and decided he was fine. She face-rubbed a couple of six and seven-year-olds into submission. She gets along great with the two adult cats in her foster home. She just needs someone willing to let her do things at her pace.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue org based in the Atlanta area. Head to their website to learn more about Judy and submit an inquiry to get the process started.
shelter_address: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue â Kennesaw, GA (foster-based rescue; no public facility)
scoring_notes:
â¢ Heart condition is well-monitored and manageable, but may give adopters pause â worth addressing head-on in the newsletter
â¢ Emotionally resonant underdog angle: last of her litter still waiting while siblings all found homes
â¢ Strong compatibility profile (kids, cats, dogs) makes her a broadly appealing candidate despite the medical footnote

blurb:
Judy's siblings all found homes first. She stayed behind, not because anything is wrong with her, but because she came with a little extra information attached. She was born with a heart condition called ventricular septal defect, which is a small hole between the lower chambers of her heart. She's had two echocardiograms and is doing well. You would genuinely not know it by watching her.

She's about a year old, around 9.5 pounds, and playtime is her main thing. Feather wands, string toys, chasing whatever moves. She also plays on her own when you're busy, so she doesn't need constant entertainment. When she's done, she finds a soft spot and curls up. Pretty ideal setup for a working household.

She's been living with two adult cats in her foster home and loves them both. She met a dog for the first time recently, took a minute to think it over, then went nose-to-nose with him and decided he was fine. Two kids ages 6 and 7 earned an enthusiastic face rub of approval from her. She can warm up to just about anyone. She just asks that introductions go at her pace, not yours.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue based in Kennesaw. Head to their website to learn more about Judy and submit an inquiry to get the process started.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
â¢ Heart condition spooked adopters while siblings got placed first â classic overlooked-pet urgency
â¢ Medically documented, vet-monitored, and behaviorally stellar with cats, dogs, and young kids
â¢ Long wait despite zero behavioral red flags makes her the newsletter's most compelling underdog story

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old and has already done the work. She went through a prison foster training program, came out knowing how to trust people, and is now in a foster home where her foster mom says adopters will hit the jackpot. That is not rescue marketing copy. That is someone who lives with this dog and means it.

She is a 58-pound Lab mix who loves her people deeply and is not shy about it. She is good with cats and has warmed up to dogs she has met at the rescue. When she first encounters a new dog she can come across as dominant, but she settles into play mode quickly. She came in a little guarded around strangers, but the training program helped a lot, and her foster has found that meeting new people outside of the home first makes a big difference. That is an easy thing to work with once you understand it.

She would do best with dog-savvy older kids, 15 and up, and any adoption with younger kids would include a meet with her trainer just to make sure everyone is comfortable. If you are looking for a loyal, loving dog who has already done the hard work of learning to trust and just needs someone to come home to, Yasmeen is the one.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Visit mostlymutts.org to see her full profile and start an inquiry.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Prison training program backstory is the most compelling and unique origin in this entire candidate set
â¢ Senior Lab mix with verified social rehabilitation â foster's jackpot claim carries earned credibility
â¢ Eight years old with a narrowed household profile signals extended wait; newsletter exposure could be the turning point

blurb:
Gina came in as part of the Go-Gos litter, got adopted, and then got returned after about a month when she started having stomach issues. Nobody figured out exactly why. She's been through several rounds of food changes and vet visits, and she's doing better, though she still vomits sometimes and the tests haven't given a clear answer. Her foster calls her the best cat ever anyway.

She's eleven months old, weighs five pounds, and is probably going to stay pretty small. If you've ever wanted a cat that keeps that kitten energy without actually being a kitten, Gina is genuinely that. She's playful and snuggly, independent enough to entertain herself, but happy to curl up with you when the moment is right.

Because she's on a special diet, she'd do best as your only cat unless you can commit to feeding her separately. That's a real logistical thing to think about, but it's not a dealbreaker if you're organized about it. She's done well with the cats in her foster home and wasn't bothered by a leashed dog. She likes kids too, as long as they're gentle and know how to handle a small cat.

Mostly Mutts is a foster-based rescue in the Atlanta area. Visit their website to read more about Gina and submit an adoption inquiry.
shelter_address: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue â Kennesaw, GA (foster-based rescue; no public facility)
scoring_notes:
â¢ Return-and-medical-mystery arc creates a reader-rooting moment â she needs the right person to finally stick
â¢ Special diet requirement narrows the adopter pool, but the right organized, cat-only household is a real match
â¢ Time in limbo â adopted, returned, still unresolved â signals genuine urgency for a second-chance placement

blurb:
Yasmeen spent time in a prison foster and training program before landing in a foster home, and her foster mom says anyone who adopts her is going to hit the jackpot. She's eight years old, 58 pounds, and the kind of dog who takes a little time to trust you and then loves you completely.

When she first arrived, she was unsure of new people. That changed fast. In the training program it took about a day before she was fully relaxed and bonded. She's good with cats, likes other dogs once the initial meeting is behind her, and gave kisses to a seven-year-old on a day trip with her foster. She does have a protective streak with her people when strangers come into her space, which is manageable as long as new people meet her outside the home first rather than walking straight into her territory. The rescue recommends that approach and it's been working well.

For a family looking for a loyal, calm, loving dog who just wants to be your person, Yasmeen is ready. She'd do best with older kids, ideally 15 and up, and a family that understands a dog who guards is not a bad dog, just a devoted one.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Adoptions go through their website at mostlymutts.org, where you can read her full profile and submit an inquiry.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Prison training program backstory plus senior-dog loyalty arc is the most compelling narrative in this group â built for newsletter readers who respond to depth
â¢ Eight years old with a slow-to-trust personality significantly extends her likely shelter timeline, making her a high-urgency feature candidate
â¢ Guardian behavior is framed clearly and sympathetically â gives the right reader confidence rather than concern, and appeals to experienced dog owners

blurb:
Gina came in as part of a litter, got adopted, and then got returned. Not because she was difficult or destructive, but because she started having stomach issues and her family couldn't figure it out. Mostly Mutts took her back, spent months working through food changes and vet visits, and she's doing better. She may still vomit occasionally and the tests haven't pinpointed a cause. That's just part of her deal.

She's 11 months old and weighs about 5 pounds. She'll likely stay small. Her foster calls her the best cat ever, and given everything Gina has been through, that says a lot. She's independent enough to do her own thing, but she does love to snuggle when the moment is right. Playful, sweet, and still has plenty of kitten energy despite everything.

Because she's on a special diet, she'd do best as an only cat unless you're willing to manage separate feeding times. She got along fine with a leashed dog and has been good with kids in her foster home. She just needs someone who's patient and paying attention. If you've had a pet with dietary sensitivities before, you already know the routine.

Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. Visit their website to submit an inquiry about Gina and set up a meet.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
â¢ Returned, medically managed, and still charming â a redemption arc readers will root for
â¢ Foster's 'best cat ever' endorsement after months of intensive care is a rare and credible signal
â¢ Dietary complexity narrows the pool but creates a targeted call-to-action for experienced pet owners

blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control and landed in a foster home where she has been absolutely thriving. She is two years old, about 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and sweet. She is also, by all accounts, a dog who will absolutely notice when you come home and will want you to know she noticed.

She is a Velcro dog in the best way. She wants to be near you, wants your attention, and has learned to dial it back when asked. She plays great with the three dogs in her foster home, is a solid alert dog (nothing gets past her), and has been doing great through the night without any potty breaks needed.

There is one thing worth knowing up front: Jacquelyn has a congenital kidney defect. It is manageable and has not slowed her down at all, but it does mean she will likely have a shorter lifespan than most dogs, and she does best in a home where someone is around during the day for a few potty breaks. She would do best without cats and probably without toddlers, though meeting older kids is totally on the table. If you work from home and have a dog-friendly household, she is absolutely worth going to meet.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can browse her full profile and submit an adoption inquiry on their website at mostlymutts.org.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Congenital kidney defect and no-cat/no-toddler requirements demand a niche adopter but create powerful emotional urgency
â¢ Thriving in foster despite a shortened prognosis â a love-while-you-can narrative that resonates deeply
â¢ Work-from-home culture makes her day-companion needs more achievable than ever for today's adopter pool

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a little nubby tail, and an energy level her foster puts somewhere between a 7 and an 8. She is a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and she is looking for someone who actually wants to do things. She was adopted once before and came back because the activity level was not a match. That is honest information and it matters. She is not a couch dog. She is an adventure dog who then becomes a couch dog, and that is a specific kind of wonderful if it fits your life.

She went through the Mostly Mutts prison training program and came out sweet, friendly, and genuinely fun to be around. She does some mouthy play when she is excited, never mean, just enthusiastic, which is worth knowing if you have younger kids. Her foster thinks 10 and up would be a good fit. She does best as an only dog and is not great with cats, so she needs a home set up for her to be the one.

If you have the energy to match hers and space to run around, she will pay you back in full on the couch at the end of the day.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Head to mostlymutts.org to read her full profile and get the adoption process started.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Returned for activity mismatch plus only-dog and no-cat requirements create real placement urgency for a mid-age hound
â¢ Prison training program grad with a vivid, honest personality portrait â adventure dog by day, couch dog by night is highly shareable copy
â¢ Six years old with a return on record; right adopter match will close fast once the story reaches active lifestyle readers

blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control and landed in a foster home where she has been absolutely thriving. She's about two years old, 58 pounds, and the kind of dog who wiggles her way into your lap before you even sit down. She's not sneaky about it either. She just loves people and wants to be close to them.

Her foster describes her as lovable, playful, silly, and sweet. She knows sit, back, and no, and she plays well with the three dogs in her foster home. She's also a serious alert dog. Nothing gets past her, and once she tells you about it, a simple acknowledgment is all she needs to settle back down. She'd do best in a home without cats and probably without toddlers, since she's got enough enthusiasm to accidentally send a small kid flying. Older kids, though, she'd love.

One thing worth knowing: Jacquelyn has a congenital kidney defect. It's manageable, her foster says it hasn't changed her day-to-day routine much at all, and she's been perfectly clean through the night. She may have a shorter lifespan than some dogs, but right now she is a happy, healthy girl who is fully living her life. She'd do best with someone home during the day for a few mid-day potty breaks.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can learn more and start the adoption process on their website at mostlymutts.org.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Congenital kidney defect is a hard stop for some readers but opens a meaningful 'love fiercely while you can' narrative that resonates deeply
â¢ Enthusiastic, people-obsessed personality makes her instantly likable â the right home exists and needs to find her
â¢ No-cats, no-toddlers, home-during-day requirements limit the pool and increase urgency for a targeted featured placement

blurb:
Caspian is an eight-month-old tuxedo cat who came to Mostly Mutts with help from a Good Samaritan and has spent his time in foster care being exactly the kind of cat that makes people text their friends unprompted. He's sweet, he's social, he wants to be wherever you are, and his favorite spot is your lap.

His foster describes him as affectionate and gentle with adults and kids alike. He's not a cat who's going to make you work for it. He's friendly from the jump and the kind of presence in a home that you just feel good around. He plays when he's in the mood and settles in when he's not. He also has strong opinions about treats.

He gets along well with the other cats in his foster home, so if you've already got a cat at home, this could work well. His reaction to dogs is unknown, so if you have one, a meet and greet would be the right move. He's litter trained and keeps his space clean.

Mostly Mutts is a foster-based rescue in the Atlanta area. Head to their website to read more about Caspian and start the adoption process.
shelter_address: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue â Kennesaw, GA (foster-based rescue; no public facility)
scoring_notes:
â¢ Near-frictionless adoption candidate: lap cat, friendly from day one, good with cats and kids â lowest barrier to placement in this group
â¢ Story is thin but the personality is genuinely charming and easy to sell in a short newsletter format
â¢ Young, uncomplicated, and likely moving fast â best featured as a quick win to drive adoption traffic to the rescue

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a little nubby tail, and enough energy to make a previous adopter tap out. That family wasn't the right fit. She's back with Mostly Mutts now and looking for someone who actually wants an adventure partner, not just a dog to hang out on the couch with. She'll get there eventually, but she wants to earn it first.

She's six years old, 35 pounds, and her foster rates her energy at a seven or eight. She's playful, affectionate, loves being around people, and does some gentle mouthy play when she gets excited. Not aggressive, just enthusiastic. She went through the Mostly Mutts prison training program and has learned a lot. She's also sweet in a really specific way: when they bathed her at the shelter, she was nervous at first, and by the time they got to cleaning her ears she was giving kisses.

Josie needs to be the only pet in the home, no dogs or cats. She'd probably do well with kids ten and up who match her energy. If you're the kind of person who wants a dog for hikes, runs, or long walks and then a couch companion at the end of the day, Josie is genuinely that dog.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Visit mostlymutts.org to see her full profile and get the adoption process started.
scoring_notes:
â¢ Return story plus only-pet requirement creates real placement urgency â she's been passed over once and has a narrow but passionate ideal-adopter profile
â¢ Prison training program plus the bath-and-kisses detail gives writers a warm, specific moment that makes her stick in readers' minds
â¢ Active-lifestyle angle is a strong differentiator â hiker and runner households are an underserved adopter segment worth speaking to directly
